data.title: Highly anticipated meeting on the future of FEMA canceled
data.description:
A highly anticipated meeting at which the Trump administration was expected to reveal its plans to reform the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) has apparently been canceled.

A Federal Register notice instructed members of the public who wanted to view the meeting of the administration’s FEMA Review Council to register to receive a link to view it. When a reporter for The Hill did so, they did not receive a link.

At the meeting, members of the council, led by Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, were expected to unveil their recommendations and vote on whether to advance them to President Trump.

A spokesperson for Virginia Gov. Glenn Youngkin (R), one of the members of the review council, confirmed to The Hill that the meeting was canceled. The reasons for the cancellation were not immediately clear.

A spokesperson for the Defense Department referred The Hill to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which houses FEMA. A spokesperson for DHS referred The Hill to the White House, which did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The cancellation comes amid reported tensions between Noem and the White House, though Trump has denied such reports. 

Noem drew Democratic criticism when she left a congressional hearing early, saying she had to attend the FEMA meeting, which had been slated for 1 p.m. 

“I have to actually leave this hearing early, because the FEMA review Council is giving their report today on suggestions for changes to FEMA. I have to co chair it, but I will be leaving soon to have to go do that,” Noem said.

—Rebecca Beitsch contributed.

data.title: Hungary’s Orban: Trump understands ‘Europe’s decline’
data.description:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán on Thursday said President Trump understands “Europe’s decline” and can see “that Europe has hit the wall of a long economic dead end.”

The remarks come after Trump released his National Security Strategy (NSS) last Thursday that accuses the European Union of stifling “political liberty” and seeks U.S. alignment with “patriotic European parties,” which generally share Trump’s anti-immigrant point of view.

Trump has also criticized Europe’s leaders for a “lack of self-confidence” that has contributed to a deteriorating relationship with Russia. European powers have resisted Trump’s efforts to force a Russia-friendly peace deal on Ukraine.

Orbán echoed Trump on both points in a post Thursday on the social media platform X.

“In summary: America has a precise understanding of Europe’s decline,” Orbán wrote. “They see the civilisational-scale decline that we in Hungary have been fighting against for fifteen years. At last, we are not fighting against it alone.”

“A weak ally cannot defend itself and cannot be relied upon in international affairs either,” Orbán continued. “They see Europe’s civilisational crisis as well. They see that Europe’s civilisational values, democracy, and the free market are all in danger.”

The Hungarian leader claimed that “European liberals have burned the network of relations that once existed with Russia, which was a mistake.”

“According to American decision makers, Europe’s relationship with Russia needs to be rebuilt at a strategic level,” Orbán wrote.

Orbán is considered Russian President Vladimir Putin’s closest ally among the European Union (EU) leaders and one of the few EU countries to import large quantities of Russian fossil fuels.

Moscow has embraced Trump’s new National Security Strategy, which does not cast Russia as a threat to U.S. interests.

Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) ripped the strategy on Europe for lecturing “European allies & [embracing] authoritarian leaders who enrich the Trump family & their billionaire friends.”

Trump accused Europe’s leaders of being “weak” and called the continent’s nations “decaying” in an interview with Politico conducted on Monday.

“I think they’re weak, but I also think that they want to be so politically correct,” Trump told Politico’s Dasha Burns. “I think they don’t know what to do. Europe doesn’t know what to do. They don’t know what to do on trade either. I mean, I look at a lot of the trade, you know, situation that’s going on over there. It’s a little bit dangerous.”

These remarks prompted European Council President António Costa to urge Trump to “respect the democratic choices of our citizens.”

“When all the leaders elect me President of the European Council, President Trump must respect this,” Costa said. “As we respect that American citizens elected him President of the United States. It is like this — that allies behave with each other.”

Europe’s leaders also rallied behind Ukraine earlier this week after Trump ramped up pressure on Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to accept a peace deal. On a conference call with other European leaders on Monday, French President Emmanuel Macron said there’s a chance that the U.S. could “betray” Ukraine, according to a leaked transcript obtained by the German outlet Spiegel.

“We mustn’t leave Ukraine and Volodymyr alone with these guys,” Finnish President Alexander Stubb said during the conversation with Rutte, Merz and Macron.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer echoed this sentiment while hosting Macron and Zelensky in London on Monday, proclaiming that the United Kingdom stands with Ukraine.

data.title: Cuba condemns US seizure of oil tanker
data.description:
Cuba’s top diplomat on Wednesday called the U.S. Coast Guard’s (USCG) recent seizure of an oil tanker an “aggressive escalation” amid growing tensions between the Trump administration and Venezuela.

“We condemn the vile act of piracy and the seizure by that country’s military forces of a vessel carrying Venezuelan oil, which contravenes the rules of free trade and freedom of navigation, in open violation of International Law,” Cuban Minister of Foreign Affairs Bruno Rodriguez Parrilla posted on the social platform X.

In a subsequent X post on Thursday, Parilla shared a statement from Bolivarian Alliance for the Peoples of Our America, which branded the seizing of the tanker an “act of piracy.”

The USCG led an operation on Wednesday to board and seize the vessel. Coast Guard members hopped on a helicopter from the aircraft carrier USS Gerald R. Ford — the Pentagon’s largest aircraft carrier stationed in the Caribbean earlier this year — and boarded the tanker.

Attorney General Pam Bondi a posted video of the seizure and said the tanker was used to “transport sanctioned oil from Venezuela and Iran.”

After President Trump announced the seizure, he did not elaborate on why other than to say the tanker “was seized for a very good reason.” The president suggested that the oil aboard the tanker now belongs to the U.S.

Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro’s government also alleged the USCG engaged in an act of piracy and accused Trump of orchestrating a “deliberate plan to plunder our energy resources,” according to a translated statement.

“In these circumstances, the true reasons for the prolonged aggression against Venezuela have finally been revealed. It is not migration. It is not drug trafficking. It is not democracy. It is not human rights,” the statement continued. “It has always been about our natural wealth, our oil, our energy, the resources that belong exclusively to the Venezuelan people.”

The seizure of the tanker is one of the latest developments amid escalating tensions between the two countries. The U.S. has carried out a series of strikes on alleged drug-smuggling boats in the Caribbean. The attacks have stirred controversy involving Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s reported orders over the initial strikes on Sept. 2 — which have killed 87 people.

The U.S. has also amassed a large military presence in Latin American waters. Trump has said the military will carry out action on Venezuelan soil “very soon.”

Democratic lawmakers slammed the latest moves, with Sen. Chris Coons (D-Del.) on Tuesday condemning the president for “sleepwalking us into a war.”

data.title: These states would benefit most from ‘Trump accounts’ for kids, report finds
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Next summer, qualifying children and newborns will be able to start saving for their adult years through so-called “Trump accounts.”

Who qualifies for those savings accounts will vary, as will the impact they may have per state, according to a new report.

Personal finance site SmartAsset recently analyzed states to determine, based on current fertility and replacement rates, which could have the most children in the coming years. SmartAsset considered each state’s population of women between the ages of 20 and 34 and compared it with the associated fertility rate for the same age group to project how many babies may be born annually using U.S. Census Bureau data.

While it may not serve as an exact representation of how many “Trump accounts” will be established (parents can also opt out), the estimates show which states may have the greatest pool of eligible recipients.

The analysis found that Utah is expected to see the greatest benefit from the “Trump accounts.” The state, well-known for its large families, has a high fertility rate among young women (97 births per 1,000) and the greatest prevalence of young women, who comprise roughly 12% of the population. According to SmartAsset, this gives Utah an annual projection of more than 34,000 births.

The interactive map below, courtesy of SmartAsset, shows the estimated per-capita replacement rate. Hovering over or clicking on a state will also show projected annual birth rates, prevalence of young women, and forecasted births per 1,000 young women.

While Utah has the highest fertility rate among young women, the most populous states are, as expected, estimated to have the most babies born annually.

Based on SmartAsset’s analysis, California is projected to see more than 344,000 babies born annually, followed by Texas (278,232) and Florida (180,880). New York, Illinois and Pennsylvania may also see more than 100,000 newborns each year.

Alternatively, states expected to see the fewest babies born yearly include Wyoming (4,491), Vermont (4,884) and Alaska (6,426).

You can view SmartAsset’s full report here.

Established as part of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed earlier this year, the “Trump accounts” are intended to give children a savings fund for certain expenses once they turn 18.

The accounts, which have to be opened by a parent or guardian, will invest in the stock market as the child grows. Funds will be managed by private banks and brokerages and need to be invested in “certain mutual funds or exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500 or another index of primarily American equities,” according to the IRS.

They will be treated much like individual retirement accounts, or IRAs, and there are few limits on the contributions that can be made to the accounts.

Any child in the U.S. with a Social Security number can get a “Trump account,” but there are age limits on who qualifies for the available startup funds.

For children born between Jan. 1, 2025, and Dec. 31, 2028, the U.S. Treasury will make a one-time deposit of $1,000. A recent donation of $6.25 billion will fund $250 contributions to roughly 25 million children, ages 10 and younger, who are too old to qualify for the $1,000 deposit.

Children who fall in the latter group must live within a zip code where the median household income is under $150,000. Any remaining funds may be disbursed to children older than 10.

The first contribution to “Trump accounts” cannot be made until after July 4. If you have a child who qualifies, you will be able to complete a Form 4547. In May, parents who sign up will get information about how to finish opening the accounts. Beginning in July, the White House says, it will have a website where parents can register for the accounts.

Funds cannot be withdrawn until the child turns 18 and are intended to be used by the beneficiary to put toward their education, buying a home, or starting a business.

More details about the accounts are expected to be released later this month, according to a website established for the “Trump accounts.”

data.title: More ships in Venezuela at risk of seizure
data.description:
Dozens of tankers are transporting American-sanctioned oil off the coast of Venezuela, raising the possibility of further seizures after the U.S. captured an oil tanker in the Caribbean on Wednesday.

More than 80 vessels loaded or waiting to load oil were in Venezuelan waters or near its coast as of Wednesday, including over 30 under U.S. sanctions, according to Reuters.

U.S. forces captured the oil tanker off the coast of Venezuela. Attorney General Pam Bondi released a video of the seizure on the social platform X, adding that the FBI, Homeland Security Investigations, the U.S. Coast Guard and the Pentagon executed a warrant on the tanker, which the U.S. said was transporting sanctioned oil from Venezuela and Iran.

The oil was sanctioned due to its “involvement in an illicit oil shipping network supporting foreign terrorist organizations,” Bondi added. The attorney general noted that the federal government’s investigation to “prevent the transport of sanctioned oil continues.”

The move marked the latest escalation by the administration toward Venezuela and its leader, Nicolás Maduro, who the State Department alleges is the head of a drug-trafficking terrorist network. 

The Pentagon has built up a significant presence in the U.S. Southern Command region, sending warships, Marines, fighter jets, spy planes and other assets. Trump has also authorized over 20 strikes on alleged drug-trafficking boats in the Caribbean and eastern Pacific since early September and confirmed in October that he had authorized the CIA to conduct operations in Venezuela.

Democratic lawmakers and libertarian Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), a frequent critic of the administration’s posture toward Venezuela, expressed concern to NewsNation’s Hannah Brandt that the seizure will further increase tensions with the South American countries. 

The Venezuelan government, meanwhile, said in a translated statement that the seizure “constitutes a blatant a theft and an international piracy.”

Maduro’s government also vowed to “defend with absolute determination its sovereignty, its natural resources, and its national dignity.”

data.title: Russia demands Trump administration provide reasoning for seizure of oil tanker
data.description:
Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov on Thursday demanded that the Trump administration explain why Venezuela’s oil tanker was seized by U.S. forces.

“I really hope that the United States, although they consider themselves entitled to conduct such operations, will somehow explain, out of respect for other members of the world community, what facts led them to take such actions,” he said during an ambassadors’ roundtable on the Ukrainian crisis resolution.

Attorney General Pam Bondi has already shared a defense for the Wednesday move alleging that the oil onboard was sanctioned due to its “involvement in an illicit oil shipping network supporting foreign terrorist organizations.”

She said Wednesday that oil was being transported from Venezuela and Iran.

However, global counterparts have questioned the move conducted by U.S. military forces without advanced notice after repeated strikes on alleged “narco-terrorist” ships traveling in the Caribbean.

“There is too little information here because I do not know how the United States views the Venezuelan situation, except that President [Donald] Trump has spoken publicly demanding a regime change or voluntary resignation of [Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro], but Chevron is operating in Venezuela, buying Venezuelan oil,” Lavrov said, according to the translation from Russia’s Tass news agency.

“What illegal volumes of this type of fuel were on this tanker – we need to get to the bottom of that somehow,” he added.

On Wednesday, Trump did not provide additional details about the seizure, and he told reporters they would hear from “the appropriate people” about it.

“It was seized for a very good reason,” Trump said.

Asked what would happen to the oil on the tanker, Trump said, “I assume we’re going to keep the oil.”

In recent weeks, the Trump administration has increased its military presence in the U.S. Southern Command area, sending warships, Marines, fighter jets, spy planes and other assets, arguing the buildup is necessary to defend the U.S.  from an influx of narcotics.

data.title: Union bosses sue to defend system that helped turn federal prison into ‘rape club’
data.description:
Thirty years ago, three women incarcerated at the federal prison in Dublin, Calif., were sexually assaulted after guards allegedly permitted male prisoners to go into their cells in the middle of the night. Three years later, in 1998, the women won a large settlement. But reports of outrageous sexual abuse and harassment of Federal Correctional Institution-Dublin inmates did not stop.

Just since 2021, more than 100 women have come forward to accuse eight former FCI Dublin corrections officers of criminal abuses, such as fingering their private parts, bribing them with cellphones in exchange for sex, and entering into their cells while they were naked. Seven of the accused former guards have pleaded guilty or been convicted by a jury.

How did FCI Dublin, which was recently shuttered, become infamous as a “rape club”? 

There’s ample evidence that one of the key villains in the FCI Dublin horror is the cynically mislabeled Civil Service Reform Act, which was rammed through a Big Labor Congress in 1978. The law statutorily grants federal union bosses “exclusive representation” power to block executive branch officials who are appointed by and accountable to the president from deciding on their own how federal civil servants are managed and disciplined.

Until it was closed last year, Ed Canales was the longtime president of FCI Dublin’ Union’s local of the American Federation of Government Employees. In that capacity, he wielded monopoly-bargaining power over guards at this facility, which housed 600-760 female prisoners. 

Canales grossly abused that power to “enable” correctional officers who were sexually abusing incarcerated women and to sweep “their alleged misconduct under the rug,” according to multiple sources cited by San Francisco’s KTVU in a report that aired in April.

This spring, Canales took to the stand to testify as a defense witness for Darrell Wayne Smith, the last of the accused former FCI Dublin guards to be tried in court. Perhaps because of Canales’ testimony depicting Smith as a victim, the jury ultimately deadlocked and a mistrial was declared.

Former prison unit manager Tess Korth, Smith’s supervisor, had informed Canales that Smith was indeed a bad officer, according to KTVU’s reporting. She asked that the union stop “getting him out of trouble.” Canales responded that, while he “hated dirty officers,” he had to “go after management to get the process right.”

Canales has denied this allegation, which he calls “insulting.”

Government union bosses wielding monopoly-bargaining privileges have on multiple occasions “frustrated and undermined accountability” in the oversight of federal prisoners, as even David Fathi of the generally pro-Big Labor American Civil Liberties Union has acknowledged.

That’s why President Trump’s executive order from March is so important — it freed large swaths of the federal workforce from union bosses’ obstructionist power.

In September, Trump-appointed Director of the Federal Bureau of Prisons William K. Marshall exercised his authority under this order to terminate union monopoly bargaining with the bosses of the American Federation of Government Employees across the country — a vital step toward greater accountability.

While the Civil Service Reform Act authorizes monopolistic unionism in federal workplaces, it also authorizes presidents to exempt employees engaged in defending “national security.” That is the legal basis for the executive order and Marshall’s implementation of it.

Chandra Bozelko, a journalist who focuses on criminal justice and prisons, affirmed in an Oct. 4 commentary for the Wall Street Journal that the union subsidiary that wields “exclusive” representation privileges over more than 30,000 federal corrections officers — the Council of Prison Locals — is “an obstacle to accountability and inmate safety.”

In principle, Americans of all political stripes would undoubtedly agree that sexual abuse, harassment and retaliation should never be part of any federal prison sentence. Unfortunately, the union bosses have already made it clear that perpetuating their special privileges is more important to them than restoring integrity and order to federal prison management.

In late September, Council of Prison Locals President Brandy Moore White declared she and her colleagues were prepared to “take every legal and legislative action necessary” to reinstate their monopoly bargaining powers. Sure enough, in late November, the council sued the Trump administration to defend the very privileges that were largely responsible for turning FCI Dublin into a “rape club.”

Greer is the senior research associate with the National Institute for Labor Relations Research.

data.title: Trone runs for old Maryland House seat
data.description:
Former Rep. David Trone (D-Md.), who ran and lost for a Senate seat in Maryland last year, is launching a bid for his old House seat in Maryland’s 6th Congressional District as he looks to make a comeback to Congress next year.

Trone filed paperwork with Federal Election Commission (FEC) on Thursday announcing his intent to run in the district, which includes much of the state’s northern border and cities like Hagerstown and Frederick. 

“We need someone that can really be a fighter,” Trone told The Washington Post in an interview published Thursday. He will be challenging Rep. April McClain Delaney (D-Md.), who he said was “not doing the job” and “not fighting Trump.”

Trone, the co-founder of the alcoholic retailer Total Wine & More, spent tens of millions last cycle in a bid for former Sen. Ben Cardin’s (D-Md.) seat, ultimately losing to Sen. Angela Alsobrooks (D-Md.) in the Democratic primary. 

Trone’s decision to launch a bid for the seat tees up a contentious and likely expensive primary clash against McClain Delaney, who’s serving her first term. 

In a statement on X, the first-term House member suggested she’s not planning to step aside and retire after her current term and pushed back against Trone’s assertation that she wasn’t fighting the president. 

“Now David Trone says I should step aside so he can have his old office back after he ran for the Senate, lost and has been sitting on the sidelines,” McClain Delaney said in an ad responding to Trone’s announcement. “He’s a distraction.” 

2024 Election Coverage

  • 2024 Election News
  • 2024 Election Polling Data
  • 2024 Presidential Election Forecast

The seat went for former Vice President Harris in 2024 by close to 6 points. Though the seat leans Democratic and the party is favored to have a good environment next year, a primary clash adds headaches as they’re looking to take back the House majority next year.

Trone’s announcement comes as a wave of younger Democratic candidates are primarying incumbents, arguing that some in the party have been in Congress too long or are not doing enough to fight against Trump. The former congressman’s entry, albeit is expected to be well-funded, may be running into challenging competing cross-political winds.

One variable that could complicate things further is whether Maryland decides to redistrict. Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) appears keen on moving forward with trying to pass an aggressive 8-0 Democratic-favored map, though Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson (D) has expressed his opposition to the idea.

data.title: La Niña likely for next month, NOAA says. Here’s what it means for winter weather
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – La Niña hasn’t left us just yet. The climate phenomenon is expected to “continue for the next month or two,” national forecasters said in an update Thursday.

This year’s La Niña, which officially arrived on the scene in October, is favored to last through most of the 2025-2026 winter before dissipating sometime early next year, according to the Climate Prediction Center, part of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).

In the meantime, the climate pattern continues to influence the type of weather we’ll see this winter. Typically, La Niña keeps weather dry and warmer in the southern half of the country, making drought conditions worse in Southern California and the Southwest. On the other hand, the Pacific Northwest and Ohio Valley tend to see more precipitation during a La Niña winter.

That pattern matches up pretty closely with what the Climate Prediction Center is predicting for the next three months: more precipitation – which could be rain or snow – for the northern states, but less-than-average precipitation down south.

La Niña generally divides the country into two fates, but the dividing line moves year to year. That uncertainty shows in the forecast map below, with the middle of the country having equal chances of above-average, below-average and normal precipitation.

The temperature outlook, similarly, divides the country. Northern states are favored to see colder-than-normal weather this winter, according to the long-range forecast. States shaded in orange, especially Arizona, New Mexico and Florida, are favored to see a warmer-than-average winter.

In the nearer term, a dozen states were under a Winter Weather Advisory on Thursday. Heavy snow was falling in parts of the Midwest and Pennsylvania while freezing rain threatened the morning commute in parts of the Plains states. Lake effect snow was also in the forecast for Western New York.

The Pacific Northwest was also being hit by a series of powerful storms. Residents in parts of Oregon and Washington grappled with power outages, flooding and school closures, while drivers navigated debris slides and water that closed roads and submerged vehicles. Forecasters warned that the worst was still to come, with some major rivers expected to crest later in the week.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Republicans block bill to extend expiring health insurance subsidies
data.description:
Senate Republicans on Thursday blocked a motion to advance a Democrat-drafted bill to extend enhanced health insurance premium subsidies that are due to expire at the end of 2025, tax credits that Democrats say are needed to keep premiums from rising by double-digits next year.

The legislation, which needed 60 votes to advance, failed by a vote of 51 to 48.

Republican Sens. Susan Collins (Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) voted for the Democratic plan to extend the enhanced health insurance tax credits, even though both lawmakers have called for reforms to the program. Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-Alaska), who is up for re-election next year, and Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) also voted for the Democratic proposal. 

The unusual vote on a Democratic-crafted health care plan while Republicans control the Senate was set up by a deal Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) struck with centrist Democrats to end a 43-day government shutdown.

The proposal, unveiled by Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.), would have extended the enhanced subsidies, which were first enacted during the COVID-19 pandemic, another three years.

“Our bill is the last train to leave the station. After this, we will not have time to try again before premiums shoot through the roof next year,” Schumer said on the Senate floor before the vote.

Schumer touted the Democratic plan as a “simple extension of these health care tax credits.”

“It’s time for Republicans to join us,” he urged his colleagues.

Earlier in the day, they voted for a Republican plan to convert funding for enhanced insurance premium subsidies into contributions to health savings accounts to pay for out-of-pocket costs. That proposal failed to advance on a mostly party-line vote.

The cost of health plans on the Affordable Care Act are projected to increase by 26 percent on average in 2026, according to an analysis by KFF, a health policy research group.

Enrollees on the ACA’s marketplace who receive enhanced premium subsidies are expected to see their monthly premium payments more than double if those tax credits expire in January.

Schumer signaled that Democrats would campaign on the issue next year ahead of the midterm election. 

“The American people will not forget which way senators vote today,” he warned on the floor. 

Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-N.H.), a leading advocate for extending the enhanced subsidies, said millions of Americans will “face a crushing financial blow” when the enhanced subsidies expire.

“These credits have helped millions of families, including tens of thousands of people in New Hampshire, keep their coverage and balance their family budgets,” she said.

Shaheen has participated in bipartisan talks to extend the subsidies, but those negotiations have failed to produce a bipartisan bill that could get the 60 votes needed to advance.

Republicans criticized the Democratic bill for not including any substantial reforms, such as a GOP-backed proposal to prevent households that earn more than $200,000 a year from reaping federal subsidies or another to ensure that lower-income Americans pay something into the system.

“Apparently, they think that a three-year extension with no reforms to try and disguise the real impact of ObamaCare’s spiraling costs is actually a plan. I don’t know how you can call it a plan,” Thune said on the Senate floor.

Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.) said the enhanced subsidies send too much money to insurance companies and that more of it should go to consumers.

Cassidy teamed up with Senate Finance Committee Chair Mike Crapo (R-Idaho) to introduce a competing GOP proposal to convert the value of the enhanced premium subsidies into payments to health savings accounts for people enrolled through the ACA marketplace to offset their out-of-pocket costs.

“What Democrats want to do, they want to give 100 percent of the money to the insurance companies, and the insurance company, by law under ObamaCare, can take 20 percent of it for overhead and profit. Eighty percent of it goes to patient care,” Cassidy said on the floor before the vote.

Democrats blocked a motion to advance the Cassidy-Crapo health plan in an earlier vote Thursday.

The failure of both bills, means lawmakers likely won’t vote on another proposal to address rising health insurance costs until next year.

Centrist members on both sides of the aisle expressed hope that once the dueling plans failed on the Senate floor, the path might open for more productive bipartisan talks.

“I’m ready to come to the table and I think we would be better served if after we get through these votes we sit down and come to a compromise to address the real costs that people are facing in terms of health care, and I’m ready to do that,” said Shaheen, who spearheaded the deal to reopen the federal government last month.
